question: Can you tell me about a time when you had to learn something completely new to tackle a challenge? How did you approach it, and what did you take away from the experience?

conversation.message: You know, hearing you talk about this reminds me of how important it is to not only have the skills to execute but also the mindset to push boundaries. I’d love to explore that a little more. Can you tell me about a time when you had to learn something completely new to tackle a challenge? How did you approach it, and what did you take away from the experience?

Absolutely! One that comes to mind was when I first started working with **function calling** in LLMs. At the time, it was still a pretty new concept—OpenAI had just introduced it, and there wasn’t much documentation or best practices out there.

**Situation:** We needed our supply chain agent to reliably interact with external APIs (like weather services and carrier systems) to make real-time decisions. But LLMs were notorious for giving inconsistent outputs—sometimes they’d return malformed JSON, or the data wouldn’t match the expected schema.

**Task:** I had to figure out how to make the LLM *always* return valid, structured outputs that our system could act on without manual intervention.

**Action:** I dove into the OpenAI API docs, but more importantly, I started experimenting. I built a bunch of Pydantic models to enforce strict schemas for the tool calls, and I wrote custom validation layers to catch and retry any malformed responses. I also spent a lot of time tweaking the system prompts to guide the LLM toward the right behavior. It was a lot of trial and error, but I eventually landed on a pattern that worked.

**Result:** The tool-calling infrastructure I built reduced parsing errors to *virtually zero* in production. It became a core part of our agent’s reliability, and I even open-sourced some of the patterns I developed (like the Pydantic validation layer) for others to use. The biggest takeaway for me was that sometimes you have to be comfortable being the first one to figure something out—there’s no playbook, so you just have to experiment, iterate, and trust your instincts.

It was a steep learning curve, but it’s one of the things I love about working in AI: the field moves so fast that you’re *always* learning something new.
